name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا خَرَجُوا۟ مِنْ عِندِكَ قَالُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ مَاذَا قَالَ ءَانِفًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ (١٦)
```

```english
There are some among them who prick up their ears at you. But when they go out from your presence, they say to those who have been given knowledge, ‘What did he say just now?’ They are the ones on whose hearts Allah has set a seal, and they follow their own desires. (16)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱهْتَدَوْا۟ زَادَهُمْ هُدًى وَءَاتَىٰهُمْ تَقْوَىٰهُمْ (١٧)
```

```english
As for those who are [rightly] guided, He enhances their guidance and invests them with their Godwariness. (17)
```

```arabic
فَهَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ أَن تَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءَ أَشْرَاطُهَا ۚ فَأَنَّىٰ لَهُمْ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ ذِكْرَىٰهُمْ (١٨)
```

```english
Do they await anything except that the Hour should overtake them suddenly? Its portents have already come. When it overtakes them of what avail will the admonitions they were given? (18)
```

```arabic
فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ وَمَثْوَىٰكُمْ (١٩)
```

```english
Know that there is no god except Allah, and plead [to Allah] for forgiveness of your sin and for the faithful, men and women. Allah knows your itinerary and your [final] abode. (19)
```

```arabic
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَتْ سُورَةٌ ۖ فَإِذَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ مُّحْكَمَةٌ وَذُكِرَ فِيهَا ٱلْقِتَالُ ۙ رَأَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ نَظَرَ ٱلْمَغْشِىِّ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۖ فَأَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ (٢٠)
```

```english
The faithful say, ‘If only a surah were sent down!’ But when a conclusive surah is sent down and war is mentioned in it, you see those in whose hearts is a sickness looking upon you with the look of someone fainting at death. So woe to them! (20)
```

```arabic
طَاعَةٌ وَقَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ ۚ فَإِذَا عَزَمَ ٱلْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ (٢١)
```

```english
Obedience and upright speech.... So when the matter has been resolved upon [concerning going to war], if they remain true to Allah that will surely be better for them. (21)
```

```arabic
فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَن تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَتُقَطِّعُوٓا۟ أَرْحَامَكُمْ (٢٢)
```

```english
May it not be that if you were to wield authority you would cause corruption in the land and ill-treat your blood relations? (22)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فَأَصَمَّهُمْ وَأَعْمَىٰٓ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ (٢٣)
```

```english
They are the ones whom Allah has cursed, so He made them deaf, and blinded their sight. (23)
```

```arabic
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ أَمْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبٍ أَقْفَالُهَآ (٢٤)
```

```english
Do they not contemplate the Quran, or are there locks on the hearts? (24)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱرْتَدُّوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَـٰرِهِم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَى ۙ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ سَوَّلَ لَهُمْ وَأَمْلَىٰ لَهُمْ (٢٥)
```

```english
Indeed those who turned their backs after the guidance had become clear to them, it was Satan who had seduced them, and he had given them [far-flung] hopes. (25)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ كَرِهُوا۟ مَا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِى بَعْضِ ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرَارَهُمْ (٢٦)
```

```english
That is because they said to those who loathed what Allah had sent down: ‘We shall obey you in some matters,’ and Allah knows their secret dealings. (26)
```

```arabic
فَكَيْفَ إِذَا تَوَفَّتْهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ (٢٧)
```

```english
But how will it be [with them] when the angels take them away, striking their faces and their backs?! (27)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَآ أَسْخَطَ ٱللَّهَ وَكَرِهُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٢٨)
```

```english
That, because they pursued what displeased Allah, and loathed His pleasure. So He has made their works fail. (28)
```

```arabic
أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ أَن لَّن يُخْرِجَ ٱللَّهُ أَضْغَـٰنَهُمْ (٢٩)
```

```english
Do those in whose hearts is a sickness suppose that Allah will not expose their spite? (29)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَأَرَيْنَـٰكَهُمْ فَلَعَرَفْتَهُم بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ ۚ وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِى لَحْنِ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ (٣٠)
```

```english
If We wish, We will show them to you so that you recognize them by their mark. Yet you will recognize them by their tone of speech, and Allah knows your deeds. (30)
```

```arabic
وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ حَتَّىٰ نَعْلَمَ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ مِنكُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَنَبْلُوَا۟ أَخْبَارَكُمْ (٣١)
```

```english
We will surely test you until We ascertain those of you who wage jihad and those who are steadfast, and We shall appraise your record. (31)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَشَآقُّوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٣٢)
```

```english
Indeed those who are faithless and bar from the way of Allah and defy the Apostle after guidance has become clear to them, they will not hurt Allah in the least, and He shall make their works fail. (32)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses continue according to the previous context, and within them is an address regarding the state of those in whose hearts is a sickness, the hypocrites, and those who turned back after their belief.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ حَتَّى إِذا خَرَجُوا مِنْ عِنْدِكَ قالُوا لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ ما ذا قالَ آنِفاً** — "Among them are those who listen to you, but when they leave your presence, they say to those who have been given knowledge, ‘What did he say just now?’" (47:16) etc. The word *ānifan* (آنفاً) is an active participle (اسم فاعل) in the accusative case as an adverb of time (ظرفية) or as an object of time (مفعول فيه). Its meaning is the hour just before your current hour. It is also said to mean "this very hour." In any case, it is derived from *al-anf* (الأنف), meaning the organ [the nose], [implying the very tip or beginning of something].

His saying: **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ** — "Among them are those who listen to you." The pronoun refers to those who disbelieve. What is meant by their listening to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is their lending an ear to what he recites of the Quran and what he explains to them of the principles of knowledge (أصول المعارف) and the laws of the religion (شرائع الدين).

His saying: **حَتَّى إِذا خَرَجُوا مِنْ عِنْدِكَ** — "but when they leave your presence." The pronoun refers to the relative pronoun [those who], and the pronoun is pluralized in consideration of the meaning, just as it was singular in **يَسْتَمِعُ** ("listens") in consideration of the literal word.

His saying: **قالُوا لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ ما ذا قالَ آنِفاً** — "they say to those who have been given knowledge, ‘What did he say just now?’" What is meant by "those who have been given knowledge" are the scholars of Allah among the Companions. The pronoun in "What did he say" refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

The inquiry in their saying, "What did he say just now?" is said to be for actual information, because their immersion in arrogance, pride, and the following of whims (اتباع الأهواء) would not let them understand the truthful word, as the Exalted said: **فَما لِهؤُلاءِ الْقَوْمِ لا يَكادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ حَدِيثاً** — "What is the matter with this people that they do not almost understand any discourse?" (4:78). It is also said to be for mockery, and it is said to be for belittlement—as if the speech, being filled with falsehoods [in their view], does not yield any coherent meaning. There is a justification for each of these three meanings.

His saying: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ طَبَعَ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ** — "They are the ones on whose hearts Allah has set a seal" (47:16) is a description of them. His saying: **وَاتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ** — "and they follow their [own] desires" (47:16) is a further description after the first, functioning like an explanatory apposition (عطف التفسير). What results from this is that the following of whims is a sign of the sealing of the heart (الطبع على القلب); for the heart that is not sealed, which remains upon the purity of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — does not hesitate in understanding religious knowledge and divine realities.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا زادَهُمْ هُدىً وَآتاهُمْ تَقْواهُمْ** — "As for those who are [rightly] guided, He enhances them in guidance and invests them with their God-consciousness" (47:17). The apparent contrast between this verse and the preceding one indicates that what is meant by guidance (الاهتداء) is that which opposes the misguidance (الضلال) necessitated by the sealing of the heart; it is the submission to that which the sound primordial nature (الفطرة) guides toward and the following of the truth. The "enhancement of their guidance" from Allah, the Glorified, is His raising the degree of their faith (الإيمان). It has been previously mentioned that guidance (الهدى) and faith possess various levels. What is meant by God-consciousness (التقوى) — *the state of godwariness and protection against spiritual harm* — is that which opposes the following of whims; it is the wariness (الورع) regarding the prohibitions of Allah and the avoidance of committing sins.

Through this, it becomes clear that the enhancement of guidance refers to their perfection in the realm of knowledge (العلم), and the granting of God-consciousness refers to their perfection in the realm of action (العمل). It also appears through the contrast that the sealing of the hearts refers to their lack of perfection in knowledge, while the following of whims refers to their lack of righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) and their deprivation thereof. This does not contradict what we previously stated: that the following of whims is like an explanatory apposition to the sealing of the hearts.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَهَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً فَقَدْ جاءَ أَشْراطُها** — "Do they await anything but the Hour, that it should come upon them suddenly? Its tokens have already come" (47:18) etc. "Looking" (النظر) here means waiting (الانتظار). "Tokens" (الأشراط) is the plural of *sharaṭ* (شرط), meaning a sign (العلامة). The linguistic origin of its meaning is "condition" (الشرط) — *that upon which the existence of a thing depends* — because the realization of the condition is a sign of the realization of the thing itself. Thus, the tokens of the Hour (أشراط الساعة) are its signs that point toward it.

The context of the verse is one of sarcasm (التهكم), as if they are standing in a position where they must either follow the truth, whereby their end would be felicitous, or wait for the Hour until, when they become certain of its occurrence and are on the verge of it, they remember, believe, and follow the truth. As for following the truth today, they have not submitted to it through any proof, exhortation, or lesson. As for their waiting for the coming of the Hour so that they may remember then, it will not benefit them at all, for it comes suddenly (بغتة) and grants them no respite to prepare for it through remembrance. When it occurs, remembrance will not benefit them because that day is a day of recompense (جزاء), not a day of action (عمل). The Exalted said: **يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنْسانُ وَأَنَّى لَهُ الذِّكْرى يَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي قَدَّمْتُ لِحَياتِي** — "on that day man will take admonition, but what will the admonition avail him? He will say, ‘Alas, I wish I had sent forth [some good deeds] for my life!’" (89:23-24).

Furthermore, its tokens and signs have already come and been realized. Perhaps what is meant by its tokens is the creation of man and the division of his species into the righteous and the corrupt, the God-conscious and the profligate, which necessitates a decisive judgment (الحكم الفصل) between them and the descent of death upon them; for all of that is among the conditions for the occurrence of the Event and the coming of the Hour. It is also said that the tokens of the Hour refer to the appearance of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who is the Seal of the Prophets, the splitting of the moon, and the revelation of the Quran, which is the last of the Heavenly Books.

This is the meaning yielded by contemplation (التدبر) upon the verse, and it is—as you see—a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) even as it is presented in a sarcastic manner.

Accordingly, His saying: **بَغْتَةً** ("suddenly") is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) of the "coming," mentioned to describe the reality and to serve as the basis for His subsequent saying: **فَأَنَّى لَهُمْ إِذا جاءَتْهُمْ ذِكْراهُمْ** ("then how will their admonition avail them when it has [already] come to them?"). It is not a restriction on the "waiting" such that it would imply they are only waiting for it to come suddenly. To repel this misconception, it was said: **إِلَّا السَّاعَةَ أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً** ("anything but the Hour, that it should come upon them suddenly") and it did not say: "except that the Hour should come upon them suddenly."

His saying: **فَأَنَّى لَهُمْ إِذا جاءَتْهُمْ ذِكْراهُمْ** — "then how will their admonition avail them when it has [already] come to them?" (47:18). *Annā* (أنى) is the advanced predicate (خبر مقدم) and *dhikrāhum* (ذِكْراهُمْ) is the delayed subject (مبتدأ مؤخر), while "when it has come to them" is a parenthetical clause between them. The meaning is: How will it be possible for them to remember when it has come? That is, how will they benefit from remembrance on a day when the action performed therein does not benefit, as it is only the day of recompense.

The commentators have various opinions regarding the meaning of the sentences of the verse and its overall meaning, which we have omitted; whoever desires them should refer to their detailed books.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا اللهُ وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ** — "Know then that there is no god but Allah, and ask forgiveness for your sin and for the faithful men and faithful women" (47:19) etc. It is said that this is branched (متفرع) from all that preceded in the Surah regarding the felicity of the believers and the wretchedness of the disbelievers.

It is as if it were said: Since you know that the matter is as mentioned regarding the felicity of these and the wretchedness of those, then remain firm upon what you possess of the knowledge of the oneness of Allah (التوحيد), the Glorified. Thus, the meaning of the command to "know" (العلم) according to this is the command to remain firm (الثبات) upon knowledge.

It is also possible that it is branched from what He explained in the two preceding verses—namely His saying: **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ** ("Among them are those who listen to you") until His saying: **وَآتاهُمْ تَقْواهُمْ** ("and invests them with their God-consciousness")—regarding how He seals the hearts of the polytheists and leaves them to their sins, while reversing the matter for those who are guided to His monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and belief in Him. It is as if it were said: Since the matter is so, then hold fast to your knowledge of the oneness of the Deity and seek forgiveness for your sin and for the believers of your community, both men and women, so that you may not be among those whose hearts Allah seals and whom He deprives of God-consciousness by leaving them to their sins. This view is supported by His saying at the end of the verse: **وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ وَمَثْواكُمْ** ("And Allah knows your moving about and your abiding").

Thus, His saying: **فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا اللهُ** means, according to what the context supports: "Hold fast to your knowledge that there is no god but Allah." His saying: **وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ** ("and ask forgiveness for your sin")—the discussion regarding the meaning of "sin" (الذنب) attributed to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has preceded, and it will also come in the commentary on the beginning of Surah al-Fath, if Allah wills.

His saying: **وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ** ("and for the faithful men and faithful women") is a command to seek forgiveness for the community of believers, both men and women. It is far-removed from the Exalted that He should command the seeking of forgiveness and not meet it with forgiveness, or command a supplication and not meet it with an answer.

His saying: **وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ وَمَثْواكُمْ** — "And Allah knows your moving about and your abiding" (47:19) is a justification for what is in the beginning of the verse: "Know then that..." etc. It is apparent that *mutaqallab* (المتقلب) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) meaning the transition from one state to another, and likewise *mathwā* (المثوى) means stability and rest. The meaning is that He, the Exalted, knows all your states, whether changing or constant, motion or rest; so remain firm upon His monotheism, seek His forgiveness, and beware lest He seal your hearts and leave you to your whims.

It is also said that "moving about" and "abiding" refer to conduct in the life of this world and the residence in the Hereafter. It is said that "moving about" is the transition from the loins to the wombs, and "abiding" is the resting in the earth.

It is said that "moving about" is conduct while awake and "abiding" is sleep. It is said that "moving about" is conduct in livelihoods and earnings, and "abiding" is staying in homes. What we have previously stated is more apparent and more general.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَتْ سُورَةٌ** — "The faithful say, ‘Why has not a surah been sent down?’" (47:20) until the end of the verse. *Lawlā* (لو لا) is for instigation (تحضيضية), meaning "Why was not a Surah sent down?" whereby they manifest a desire for the revelation of a new Surah that would bring them new obligations (تكاليف) which they would fulfill. What is meant by "Surah" is a definitive (محكمة) and clear one in which there is no ambiguity (تشابه). What is meant by the "mention of fighting" is the command for it.

What is meant by "those in whose hearts is a sickness" are those weak in faith among the believers, not the hypocrites; for the verse is explicit that those who manifested the desire for its revelation were "those who believe." "Those who believe" does not encompass the hypocrites except by way of a laxity (مساهلة) that is inappropriate for the Speech of Allah. Thus, the verse is like His saying, the Exalted, regarding a group of the believers: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ كُفُّوا أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكاةَ فَلَمَّا كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقِتالُ إِذا فَرِيقٌ مِنْهُمْ يَخْشَوْنَ النَّاسَ كَخَشْيَةِ اللهِ أَوْ أَشَدَّ خَشْيَةً** — "Have you not regarded those who were told, ‘Keep your hands off [from fighting], and maintain the prayer and give the zakat’? But when fighting was prescribed for them, behold, a part of them were afraid of people as if they were afraid of Allah, or were even more afraid" (4:77).

The one "fainting at [the approach of] death" (المغشي عليه من الموت) is the one in the throes of death (المحتضر). It is said *ghashiyahu ghashāwah* (غشيه غشاوة) when something veils or covers him. *Ghashiya ‘alā fulān* (غشي على فلان)—in the passive—is said when something overcomes his intellect. The "look of one fainting at death" is his staring at you with his eyes fixed without blinking.

His saying: **فَأَوْلى لَهُمْ** — "So woe to them!" (47:20). Perhaps it is a predicate for a deleted subject, the estimation being: "That is more appropriate for them," meaning it is fitting for them to look in such a way—that is, to be in the throes of death and die. It is narrated from al-Asma‘i that their saying *awlā laka* (أَوْلى لَكَ) is a word of threat meaning "May that which you dislike befall you and accompany you." The verse is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **أَوْلى لَكَ فَأَوْلى ثُمَّ أَوْلى لَكَ فَأَوْلى** — "Woe to you! And then woe to you! Again, woe to you! And then woe to you!" (75:34-35).

The meaning of the verse is: The believers say, "Why has not a Surah been sent down?" but when a definitive Surah is sent down in which there is no ambiguity, and they are commanded therein to fight and perform jihad (الجهاد) — *the striving in the way of Allah* — you see those weak in faith among them looking at you, out of intense fear, with the look of one in the throes of death; so that is more appropriate for them.

His saying, the Exalted: **طاعَةٌ وَقَوْلٌ مَعْرُوفٌ فَإِذا عَزَمَ الْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا اللهَ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ** — "Obedience and a worthy speech. Then, when the matter is resolved, if they are true to Allah, it will surely be better for them" (47:21). "The matter is resolved" (عزم الأمر) means it has become serious and finalized.

His saying: **طاعَةٌ وَقَوْلٌ مَعْرُوفٌ** — "Obedience and a worthy speech" is as if it were a predicate for a deleted subject, the estimation being: "Our command—or their command and state—is obedience." That is, their faith in Us is an obedience they pledged to Us, and a worthy speech (قول معروف), not a reprehensible one, which they said to Us, which is the manifestation of hearing and obeying, as the Exalted recounts from them in His saying: **آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ** — "The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful [as well]..." until He said: **وَقالُوا سَمِعْنا وَأَطَعْنا** — "...and they say, ‘We hear and obey’" (2:285).

Based on this, His subsequent saying: **فَإِذا عَزَمَ الْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا اللهَ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ** ("Then, when the matter is resolved, if they are true to Allah, it will surely be better for them") connects to what preceded it with a clear connection. The meaning is: The "matter" is that which they pledged to Allah in their saying, "We hear and obey." So if, when the matter was resolved, they had been true to Allah in what they said and obeyed Him in what He commanded—including the command to fight—it would have been better for them.

It is also possible that His saying: **طاعَةٌ** ("Obedience") etc., is a predicate for a pronoun referring back to the aforementioned "fighting." The estimation would be: The fighting mentioned in the Surah is an "obedience" from them and a "worthy speech." So if, when the matter was resolved, they had been true to Allah in their faith and obeyed Him therein, it would have been better for them. As for it being an "obedience" from them, that is clear. As for it being a "worthy speech," it is because the obligation of fighting and the command to defend the righteous society to nullify the plots of its enemies is a worthy speech recognized by reason and the rational (العقلاء).

It is said that His saying: **طاعَةٌ** ("Obedience") etc., is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate is deleted, the estimation being: "Obedience and a worthy speech are better for them and more exemplary." It is also said it is a subject whose predicate is **فَأَوْلى لَهُمْ** ("So woe to them!") in the previous verse, making the verse a completion of the previous one; this is a weak opinion. Even weaker is the opinion that "Obedience" etc., is an adjective for "Surah" in His saying: "But when a [definitive] surah is sent down." Other things have also been said.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَنْ تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَتُقَطِّعُوا أَرْحامَكُمْ** — "May it not be that if you were to wield authority you would cause corruption in the land and ill-treat your blood relations?" (47:22). The address is to those in whose hearts is a sickness, who are sluggish regarding the matter of jihad in the way of Allah. The address has turned toward them (التفات) to increase the rebuke and reprimand. The inquiry is for affirmation (تقرير). "Turning away" (التولي) means averting oneself, and what is meant is averting oneself from the Book of Allah and acting upon what is in it, and returning to polytheism and rejecting the religion.

The meaning is: Is it to be expected of you, if you turn away from the Book of Allah and acting upon what is in it—including jihad in the way of Allah—that you would cause corruption in the land and sever your ties of kinship (تقطعوا أرحامكم) by shedding blood, plundering wealth, and violating honors, out of greed for the carcass of this world? That is, if you turn away, this is what would be expected of you.

Through this, it has become clear that the verse is in the position of justifying His saying in the previous verse: "it will surely be better for them," which is why it began with the particle *fā’* (ف).

It is said that "turning away" (التولي) means assuming governance and authority (الولاية). The meaning would be: Is it to be expected of you, if you are made governors, that you would cause corruption in the land and sever your ties of kinship by shedding forbidden blood, taking bribes, and being unjust in judgment? This meaning is far-removed from the context.

His saying, the Exalted: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ اللهُ فَأَصَمَّهُمْ وَأَعْمى أَبْصارَهُمْ** — "They are the ones whom Allah has cursed, so He made them deaf, and blinded their eyes" (47:23). The indication is toward those who cause corruption in the land and sever ties of kinship. Allah has described them as being cursed by Him, so He made them deaf and took away their hearing such that they do not hear the truthful word, and He blinded their eyes such that they do not see the truthful view; for it is not the eyes that go blind, but the hearts within the breasts that go blind.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ أَمْ عَلى قُلُوبٍ أَقْفالُها** — "Do they not contemplate the Quran, or are there locks on the hearts?" (47:24). The inquiry is for rebuke, and the plural pronoun refers to those mentioned in the previous verse. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "hearts" (قُلُوبٍ) is, as has been said, to indicate that what is meant are the hearts of these people and their likes.

It is said in *al-Majma‘ al-Bayan*: "In this is a proof for the invalidity of the view of those who say: It is not permissible to interpret anything from the outward meaning (ظاهر) of the Quran except through a report (خبر) and audition (سمع)." End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ ارْتَدُّوا عَلى أَدْبارِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدَى الشَّيْطانُ سَوَّلَ لَهُمْ وَأَمْلى لَهُمْ** — "Indeed those who turned their backs after guidance had become manifest to them, it was Satan who seduced them and gave them false hopes" (47:25). "Turning back on their heels" (الارتداد على الأدبار) is returning to the rear after facing forward; it is a metaphor intended to mean abandonment after acceptance. "Seduction" (التسويل) is the beautifying of that which the soul incites toward, and the depicting of the ugly to it in the form of the beautiful. What is meant by "giving false hopes" (الإملاء) is providing extension or lengthening expectations.

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قالُوا لِلَّذِينَ كَرِهُوا ما نَزَّلَ اللهُ سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِي بَعْضِ الْأَمْرِ وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرارَهُمْ** — "That is because they said to those who detest what Allah has sent down, ‘We will obey you in some matters,’ and Allah knows their secrets" (47:26). The indication "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to Satan's seduction and his giving them false hopes—in short, his mastery over them. What is meant by "those who detest what Allah has sent down" are those who disbelieve, as previously mentioned in His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَتَعْساً لَهُمْ وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** — "As for the faithless, let them be miserable, and He has made their deeds go awry. That is because they detested what Allah has sent down..." (47:8-9).

His saying: **سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِي بَعْضِ الْأَمْرِ** — "We will obey you in some matters" is the content of their speech and a promise from them to the disbelievers of obedience. As suggested by the restriction of obedience to "some matters" in a vague manner, it is the speech of one who is unable to openly manifest obedience to the one he wishes to obey in all matters, because he is in danger from manifesting absolute obedience. Thus, he secretly tells the one he promises that he will obey him in some matters and in whatever is feasible for him, then he conceals that and sits waiting for a turn of fortune.

It is inferred from this that these were a group of hypocrites who secretly conveyed to the disbelievers what the Exalted recounted from them, promising them obedience whenever it was feasible for them. This view is supported by His saying afterward: **وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرارَهُمْ** ("and Allah knows their secrets").

They have differed as to who these people were. It is said they were the Jews who said to the hypocrites: "If you announce disbelief, we will help you." It is said they were the Jews, or the Jews and the hypocrites, who said that to the polytheists. Both views are refuted by the fact that the subject of the verse is those who "turned back" (ارتدوا) after their belief, and the Jews did not believe such that they could turn back.

It is said they were the hypocrites who promised the Jews help, as the Exalted said: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نافَقُوا يَقُولُونَ لِإِخْوانِهِمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ لَئِنْ أُخْرِجْتُمْ لَنَخْرُجَنَّ مَعَكُمْ وَلا نُطِيعُ فِيكُمْ أَحَداً أَبَداً وَإِنْ قُوتِلْتُمْ لَنَنْصُرَنَّكُمْ** — "Have you not regarded the hypocrites who say to their brethren who are faithless among the People of the Book, ‘If you are expelled, we will surely go out with you, and we will never obey anyone against you; and if you are fought, we will surely help you’?" (59:11).

In this regard, the verse is capable of being applied to that, just as it is capable of being applied to the Jews in their promise of help to the polytheists—with some strain in applying "turning back" (الارتداد) to their disbelief in the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) after his messengership had become manifest to them. However, there is no evidence from the literal wording of the verse for that; perhaps they were a group of hypocrites other than them.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَكَيْفَ إِذا تَوَفَّتْهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبارَهُمْ** — "But how will it be when the angels take them away, striking their faces and their backs?" (47:27) is branched from what preceded it. The meaning is: This is their state today, turning back after guidance has become manifest to them and doing as they wish; so how will their state be when the angels take them in death while striking their faces and their backs?

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ اتَّبَعُوا ما أَسْخَطَ اللهَ وَكَرِهُوا رِضْوانَهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — "That is because they pursued what displeases Allah and detested His pleasure, so He has made their deeds go awry" (47:28). It is apparent that what is meant by "what displeases Allah" are the whims of the soul and the seductions of Satan that lead to sins and ruinous offenses, as the Exalted said: "and they follow their [own] desires," and He said: "it was Satan who seduced them and gave them false hopes."

Displeasure (السخط) and pleasure (الرضا) are among His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية), and what is meant by them is punishment and reward.

The indication in His saying: **ذلِكَ** ("That") refers to what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding the angels' punishment of them at the time of their death. That is, the cause of their punishment is that their deeds are nullified (حابطة) because of their following what displeases Allah and their detesting His pleasure; and since they have no righteous deeds, they are wretched with punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ أَنْ لَنْ يُخْرِجَ اللهُ أَضْغانَهُمْ** — "Do those in whose hearts is a sickness suppose that Allah will not expose their malice?" (47:29). Al-Raghib said: *Al-ḍighn* (الضغن)—with a *kasrah* on the *ḍād*—and *al-ḍughn* (الضغن)—with a *ḍammah*—is intense malice (الحقد الشديد), and its plural is *aḍghān* (أضغان). End quote. What is meant by "those in whose hearts is a sickness" are those weak in faith. Perhaps they are those who believed initially with a weakness in their faith, then inclined toward hypocrisy and turned back after belief. Careful contemplation of the history of early Islam clarifies that a group of those who believed in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were of this description, just as another group of them were hypocrites from the first day they believed until the end of their lives. Accordingly, their being counted among the believers in what preceded is in consideration of the beginning of their affair.

The meaning is: Rather, did these hypocrites in whose hearts is a sickness think that Allah would not bring out and manifest their malice toward the religion and its people?

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ نَشاءُ لَأَرَيْناكَهُمْ فَلَعَرَفْتَهُمْ بِسِيماهُمْ وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِي لَحْنِ الْقَوْلِ وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمالَكُمْ** — "Had We wished, We would have showed them to you and you would have recognized them by their mark; and you will surely recognize them by their tone of speech, and Allah knows your deeds" (47:30). *Sīmā* (السيماء) is the mark (العلامة). The meaning is: Had We wished, We would have shown you those with diseased hearts, and you would have recognized them by their mark which We would have made known to you.

His saying: **وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِي لَحْنِ الْقَوْلِ** — "and you will surely recognize them by their tone of speech." Al-Raghib said: *Al-laḥn* (اللحن) is turning speech away from its established course: either by removing the inflection (إعراب) or mispronunciation (تصحيف), which is blameworthy and more commonly used; or by removing it from explicitness and turning it toward allusion (تعريض) and implication (فحوى), which is praised by most men of letters in terms of eloquence. End quote.

The meaning is: You will surely recognize them from the nature of their speech by what it contains of metaphor and allusion. In making the "tone of speech" (لحن القول) the vessel for recognition, there is a type of metaphorical emphasis.

His saying: **وَاللهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمالَكُمْ** — "and Allah knows your deeds" (47:30). That is, He knows their realities and from which intentions and purposes they issued, so He will recompense the believers for their righteous deeds and others for theirs. In this is a promise for the believers and a threat for others.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ حَتَّى نَعْلَمَ الْمُجاهِدِينَ مِنْكُمْ وَالصَّابِرِينَ وَنَبْلُوَا أَخْبارَكُمْ** — "We will surely test you until We ascertain those of you who wage jihad and those who are steadfast, and We will assess your reports" (47:31). *Al-balā’* (البلاء) and *al-ibtilā’* (الابتلاء) are trial and testing. The verse is an explanation of the reason for prescribing fighting for the believers: it is the divine test (الاختبار الإلهي) to distinguish the mujahideen (المجاهدين) — *those who strive in the way of Allah* — and those who are patient (الصابرين) — *those who remain steadfast through the hardships of divine obligations*.

His saying: **وَنَبْلُوَا أَخْبارَكُمْ** — "and We will assess your reports." It is as if what is meant by "reports" (الأخبار) are the deeds, insofar as they issue from the doers and thus become reports about them. Through the testing of deeds, the righteous are distinguished from the corrupt, just as through the testing of souls, the righteous and good souls are distinguished. It has been previously mentioned that what is meant by the "knowledge" (العلم) resulting for the Exalted from testing His servants is the manifestation of the servants' state through that; and from a more precise perspective, it is an active knowledge (علم فعلي) of the Exalted that is external to the Essence.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَشَاقُّوا الرَّسُولَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الْهُدى لَنْ يَضُرُّوا اللهَ شَيْئاً وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — "Indeed those who disbelieve and bar from the way of Allah and defy the Apostle after guidance has become manifest to them, they will not hurt Allah in the least, and He will make their deeds go awry" (47:32). What is meant by these are the leaders of misguidance among the disbelievers of Mecca and those who join them, because they are the ones who barred from the way of Allah and defied the Messenger and opposed him with the most intense enmity after guidance had become manifest to them.

His saying: **لَنْ يَضُرُّوا اللهَ شَيْئاً** — "they will not hurt Allah in the least" because the plot and ruse of man only return upon himself and do not harm anyone but him. His saying: **وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — "and He will make their deeds go awry" means their efforts to demolish the foundation of the religion and what they did to extinguish the light of Allah. It is also said that what is meant is the nullification of their deeds such that they are not rewarded in the Hereafter for any of their actions. The first meaning is more suitable to the context because it contains an incitement of the believers and an encouragement for them to fight the polytheists, and a comforting of their souls that they are the victors, as the following verses indicate.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ** ("Among them are those who listen to you") etc., narrated from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah from Ali (عليه‌السلام), who said: "We were with the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and he would inform us of the revelation—so I would comprehend it, as would whoever else comprehended it—but when we left, they would say: 'What did he say just now?'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, and al-Tirmidhi narrated from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I and the Hour have been sent like these two,' and he pointed with his index and middle fingers."

I say: This wording has been narrated from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through other chains from Abu Hurayrah and Sahl ibn Mas‘ud.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn Majah, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "One day the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was appearing before the people, and a man came to him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, when is the Hour?' He replied: 'The one questioned about it is no more knowledgeable than the questioner—but I will tell you of its tokens (أشراطها).'

'When the slave-girl gives birth to her mistress, that is among its tokens. When the barefoot and naked are the shepherds of sheep and the leaders of the people, that is among its tokens. And when the shepherds of sheep compete in constructing tall buildings, that is among its tokens.'"

In *al-‘Ilal*, with its chain to Anas ibn Malik from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in a long hadith in which he says to Abdullah ibn Salam, who had asked him about several matters: "As for the tokens of the Hour (أشراط الساعة)—it is a fire that will gather the people from the East to the West."

I say: Perhaps what is meant by it is other than its outward meaning. The narrations regarding the tokens of the Hour from the paths of the Shi‘a and the Sunnis are beyond counting. The narration of Salman from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the narration of Humran from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) have already passed at the end of the fifth volume of this book, and they are two comprehensive narrations on this subject.

In *al-Majma‘*, the hadith is authentic with the chain to Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, who said: "I was a man with a sharp tongue toward my family—so I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I fear that my tongue will enter me into the Fire.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Where are you regarding the seeking of forgiveness (الاستغفار)? Indeed, I seek forgiveness from Allah a hundred times a day.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Hibban, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Agharr al-Muzani, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, my heart becomes clouded (ليغان على قلبي), and indeed I seek forgiveness from Allah every day a hundred times.'"

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ** ("May it not be that if you were to wield authority...") the verse: al-Bayhaqi narrated from Jabir ibn Abdullah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, the womb (الرحم) is suspended from the Throne (العرش); it has an eloquent tongue saying: O Allah, join the one who joins me, and sever the one who severs me.'"

I say: The narrations regarding it [the womb/kinship] and its joining and severing are many, and a portion of them has passed in the commentary on the beginning of Surah al-Nisa’.

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَلا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ** ("Do they not contemplate the Quran...") the verse: "Do they not contemplate the Quran so that they may fulfill the truth that is upon them?" Narrated from Abu Abdillah and Abu al-Hasan (عليهما السلام).

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain to Muhammad ibn ‘Umarah, who said: "I asked al-Sadiq Ja‘far ibn Muhammad (عليه‌السلام), saying to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, inform me about Allah, the Mighty and Majestic—does He have pleasure (رضى) and displeasure (سخط)?' He replied: 'Yes—but that is not like what is found in created beings. Rather, the anger (غضب) of Allah is His punishment, and His pleasure (رضاه) is His reward.'"

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِي لَحْنِ الْقَوْلِ** ("and you will surely recognize them by their tone of speech") the verse: from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, who said: "The 'tone of speech' was their hatred for Ali ibn Abi Talib." He said: "We used to recognize the hypocrites during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) by their hatred for Ali ibn Abi Talib."

The author of *al-Majma‘* said: "The like of that has been narrated from Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari."

He said: "And from ‘Ubadah ibn al-Samit, who said: 'We used to test our children with the love of Ali ibn Abi Talib—so if we saw one of them who did not love him, we knew he was not of sound birth (لغير رشدة).'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud, who said: "We did not recognize the hypocrites during the time of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) except by their hatred for Ali ibn Abi Talib."

In the *Amali* of al-Tusi, with its chain to Ali (عليه‌السلام), that he said: "I said four things for which Allah, the Exalted, sent down confirmation in His Book. I said: 'A man is hidden under his tongue; when he speaks, he appears.' So Allah sent down: **وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِي لَحْنِ الْقَوْلِ** ('and you will surely recognize them by their tone of speech')."
